seo.title: Education Data Extraction AI Agent Checklist
seo.description: Production readiness checklist for data extraction AI agents in education. Covers FERPA, COPPA, State education data privacy laws.

content.intro: Deploying AI agents for data extraction in educational environments carries significant legal and ethical obligations, particularly when handling student records protected under FERPA and COPPA. Schema-guided LLM extraction pipelines must be rigorously validated to prevent data leakage, misclassification of sensitive student information, and unauthorized disclosure to third parties. This checklist ensures your extraction architecture meets federal and state education privacy mandates while maintaining data integrity across every pipeline stage.

sections.heading: Data Governance & Consent Management
sections.description: Establish lawful basis for processing student data before any extraction pipeline ingests educational records, ensuring parental consent and institutional authorization are documented and enforceable.

items.label: Verify FERPA-compliant data sharing agreements
items.description: Confirm that a signed FERPA-compliant data sharing agreement or school official exception is in place with the institution before ingesting any education records into the extraction pipeline.

items.label: Obtain verifiable parental consent for COPPA-covered students
items.description: For students under 13, collect and log verifiable parental consent (e.g., signed forms, email confirmation) before extracting or processing any personally identifiable information (PII) from their records.

items.label: Map all extracted fields to a student data inventory
items.description: Maintain a living data inventory that maps every schema field the LLM extracts (e.g., student ID, GPA, IEP status) to its FERPA classification, retention period, and authorized recipients.

items.label: Enforce state-specific education data privacy law requirements
items.description: Audit applicable state laws (e.g., NY Education Law 2-d, California SOPIPA) and configure extraction schemas to exclude or flag fields prohibited by those statutes before pipeline deployment.

items.label: Implement data minimization in extraction schemas
items.description: Define extraction schemas that capture only the minimum necessary fields required for the stated educational purpose; remove optional schema fields that collect sensitive data without a documented use case.

items.label: Document lawful purpose for each extracted data category
items.description: For every schema field category (academic records, behavioral data, disability status), record the specific legitimate educational interest or legal basis justifying its extraction.

items.label: Establish data retention and deletion schedules for extracted outputs
items.description: Define maximum retention periods for extracted structured outputs and implement automated deletion workflows that align with institutional records retention policies and FERPA requirements.

sections.heading: Schema Validation & Extraction Accuracy
sections.description: Ensure the LLM extraction pipeline produces structurally correct, type-consistent, and complete outputs that accurately reflect source documents without hallucinating or misclassifying student data.

items.label: Enforce strict JSON schema validation on all LLM outputs
items.description: Integrate a schema validation library (e.g., Pydantic, JSON Schema validator) as a post-processing gate that rejects any LLM output failing type constraints, required field checks, or enum boundaries before downstream consumption.

items.label: Implement field-level accuracy spot-checks against source documents
items.description: For high-sensitivity fields (student ID, disability classification, grade level), run automated string-match or fuzzy-match verification against the original document to detect LLM hallucinations or misreads.

items.label: Define and enforce completeness thresholds per schema
items.description: Set minimum completeness scores (e.g., 95% of required fields populated) for each document type; route incomplete extractions to a human review queue rather than passing them to downstream systems.

items.label: Validate type consistency across batch extraction runs
items.description: Run statistical type-consistency checks across batches to detect drift where the LLM begins returning strings for numeric fields or null values for previously reliable fields, triggering pipeline alerts.

items.label: Test extraction schemas against adversarial and edge-case documents
items.description: Before production deployment, run the extraction pipeline against documents with handwriting, OCR artifacts, non-standard formatting, and multilingual content to measure schema compliance degradation.

items.label: Log all schema validation failures with source document references
items.description: Persist structured logs of every validation failure including the offending field, expected type, received value, and a reference to the source document ID for auditability and model improvement.

items.label: Version-control extraction schemas and track breaking changes
items.description: Store all schema definitions in version control and require a documented impact assessment before any breaking schema change is deployed to production extraction pipelines.

sections.heading: PII Protection & Access Controls
sections.description: Protect student PII throughout the extraction lifecycle by applying technical controls that limit exposure, enforce least-privilege access, and prevent unauthorized re-identification of anonymized data.

items.label: Redact or tokenize student PII before sending documents to LLM APIs
items.description: Apply a PII detection layer (e.g., Microsoft Presidio, AWS Comprehend) to replace student names, SSNs, and addresses with tokens before documents are transmitted to any external LLM API endpoint.

items.label: Prohibit storage of raw student documents in LLM provider infrastructure
items.description: Confirm via vendor DPA that the LLM provider does not retain input documents or extracted outputs for model training, and configure API calls with data retention opt-outs where available.

items.label: Enforce role-based access control on extracted data stores
items.description: Restrict access to extracted student data outputs using RBAC policies so only authorized staff with a documented educational need can query, export, or modify extraction results.

items.label: Encrypt extracted student data at rest and in transit
items.description: Apply AES-256 encryption to all stored extraction outputs and enforce TLS 1.2+ for all data transfers between pipeline components, document sources, and downstream consumers.

items.label: Implement audit logging for all access to extracted student records
items.description: Log every read, write, export, and deletion event on extracted student data with user identity, timestamp, and accessed record IDs to support FERPA audit requests and breach investigations.

items.label: Prevent re-identification of anonymized extraction outputs
items.description: Before releasing aggregated or anonymized extraction results, apply k-anonymity or differential privacy checks to ensure small cohort sizes cannot be used to re-identify individual students.

items.label: Conduct quarterly access reviews for extraction pipeline permissions
items.description: Schedule quarterly reviews of all service accounts, API keys, and user roles with access to the extraction pipeline and revoke permissions for accounts no longer requiring access.

items.label: Isolate extraction pipeline environments by student population sensitivity
items.description: Separate pipeline environments for documents containing IEP/504 data, disciplinary records, or health information from general academic record extraction to apply stricter controls to higher-sensitivity data.

sections.heading: Bias, Fairness & Accessibility Compliance
sections.description: Ensure the extraction pipeline does not introduce or amplify bias in student data processing and that all interfaces and outputs meet Section 508 accessibility requirements for equitable institutional use.

items.label: Audit extraction accuracy across demographic subgroups
items.description: Measure schema compliance rates and field accuracy broken down by student demographic proxies (e.g., language of origin, document format type) to detect disparate extraction performance affecting specific student populations.

items.label: Test LLM extraction prompts for demographic bias in classification fields
items.description: For fields involving subjective classification (e.g., behavioral category, learning need tier), run bias probing tests with varied demographic contexts to confirm the LLM does not produce systematically different outputs based on student identity signals.

items.label: Ensure extraction pipeline interfaces meet Section 508 accessibility standards
items.description: Audit all staff-facing dashboards, review queues, and extraction configuration tools against WCAG 2.1 AA and Section 508 criteria, including screen reader compatibility, keyboard navigation, and color contrast requirements.

items.label: Validate extraction accuracy on multilingual and non-English documents
items.description: Test schema-guided extraction on documents in languages common to the student population (e.g., Spanish, Mandarin, Arabic) and set language-specific accuracy thresholds before enabling multilingual document ingestion.

items.label: Document and disclose AI extraction use to affected students and parents
items.description: Provide plain-language disclosure in student/parent-facing communications explaining that AI is used to extract data from educational documents, what data is extracted, and how it is used.

items.label: Establish a human review escalation path for disputed extractions
items.description: Create a documented process for students, parents, or staff to flag and request human review of any extracted data they believe is inaccurate, with a defined SLA for resolution and correction.

items.label: Review extraction schemas annually for age-appropriateness of data categories
items.description: Conduct annual schema reviews with educational stakeholders to confirm that extracted data categories remain appropriate for the age groups served and do not inadvertently collect data irrelevant or harmful to minors.

notes.note: Any AI extraction pipeline that accesses, processes, or stores education records must operate under a FERPA-compliant school official exception or signed data sharing agreement. Extracted outputs containing student PII are themselves education records subject to FERPA's disclosure restrictions, meaning downstream access controls must be as stringent as those applied to the source documents.

If the extraction pipeline processes documents containing information about children under 13, the operator must obtain verifiable parental consent before collection and cannot use extracted data for any purpose beyond the stated educational service. LLM API providers used in the pipeline must also be evaluated for COPPA compliance, particularly regarding data retention and use for model training.

States including New York (Education Law 2-d), California (SOPIPA, AB 1584), and Colorado (Student Data Transparency and Security Act) impose requirements beyond FERPA, including mandatory data security plans, vendor contract clauses, and prohibitions on using student data for targeted advertising or building commercial profiles. Extraction pipeline vendors must sign state-mandated data privacy agreements before deployment.

Educational institutions receiving federal funding must ensure that AI-powered tools used by staff or made accessible to students meet Section 508 and WCAG 2.1 AA standards. This applies to extraction pipeline review interfaces, output dashboards, and any student/parent-facing disclosure or consent mechanisms associated with the AI system.

cta.heading: Automate Your Education Agent Compliance
cta.description: Use Vex to continuously monitor your AI extraction pipelines against FERPA, COPPA, and state education privacy requirements—catching schema violations, PII exposure risks, and bias signals before they become institutional liabilities.
